candidate.name: formIpQoS -> ip_qos.sh / iptables rule path
candidate.input: QoS rule fields including IP range, MAC address, bandwidth, uplink/downlink values, and comment/deviceName metadata
processing_chain: boa form handler stores or forwards QoS rule state
candidate.validation: Client-side JavaScript validates IP/MAC/port ranges and numeric bandwidth fields, but no server-side validation was confirmed in the extracted artifacts.
candidate.impact: If user-controlled QoS fields are concatenated into shell or iptables command lines, this could become authenticated firewall rule injection or command execution.

review.exploit_idea: Reverse the boa formIpQoS handler and the ip_qos.sh invocation path to determine whether comment or rule selector fields can break argument structure or shell quoting.
review.notes: Real web entrypoint and sink clues are present, but the exact runtime concatenation from form field to iptables command is not yet proven.

candidate.name: formUploadConfig -> config restore handling
candidate.input: Uploaded configuration file selected through saveconf.htm multipart form
processing_chain: HTTP multipart POST to boafrm/formUploadConfig from saveconf.htm
boa upload handler processes config restore request
firmware strings reference /config.dat and config copy/import logic
candidate.validation: The visible checks in saveconf.htm are client-side only: filename contains ProductName and suffix is .bin. Server-side validation was not confirmed beyond error strings such as Invalid configuration file.
candidate.impact: If server-side restore parsing is weak, an authenticated attacker may be able to inject crafted config state that later changes privileged runtime behavior.

review.exploit_idea: Trace formUploadConfig in boa, identify where uploaded content is written and parsed, and test whether malformed or attacker-controlled keys survive into startup scripts or service configuration.
review.notes: This is a realistic restore-attack surface, but no full config -> execution chain is proven yet. Keep it as a research lead, not a claimed vulnerability.
